message:
fcp-store: structural validation at every storage write boundary

`StoredObject::object_id` is a *keyed* derivation (HMAC-style with the
zone's `ObjectIdKey`), so the storage layer cannot independently verify
the full content-addressing invariant
  `object_id == derive_id(header, body, zone_key)`
without access to zone keys (which live in `ZoneKeyMaterial`, outside
the store). Full ID verification remains the runtime API caller's
responsibility — but the storage layer was previously skipping even
the *key-free* checks that `derive_id` would have run on the way to
producing the ID.

Net effect before this fix: a process with WAL/snapshot write access
could smuggle in a `Put(StoredObject)` whose `body` exceeded
`fcp_cbor::MAX_CANONICAL_OBJECT_BYTES` (64 MiB) or whose `header` was
not canonically CBOR-encodable (NaN/Infinity floats, duplicate map
keys, non-canonical structure). Recovery silently admitted it.

Add `StoredObject::validate_structure()` in fcp-core that runs the
canonical-bytes pipeline `derive_id` would run, returning the same
`SerializationError` for malformed or oversized objects. Wire it into:

  - `MemoryObjectStore::put` (runtime write boundary)
  - `DurableObjectState::validate_mutation` (Put branch — covers the
    live `record_mutation` path AND now the WAL replay path)
  - `DurableObjectState::from_snapshot` (snapshot recovery — propagate
    the new fallible signature through `load_durable_object_state`)
  - WAL replay loop in `load_durable_object_state` — previously called
    `apply_loaded_mutation` directly without `validate_mutation`,
    skipping every check the live path enforced. Now calls
    `validate_mutation` first, matching the symbol-store recovery path.

Regression test `wal_replay_rejects_oversized_object_body` builds a
forged WAL envelope with a 64 MiB+1 body, signs it with the correct
checksum so the bytes look authentic, and confirms recovery either
errors with "invalid object structure" or truncates the bad tail —
the forged object MUST NOT appear in the recovered store.

Verified: 12/12 durable tests green. (The `offline::access_pattern_tracker_top_n`
failure that appears under full-suite load is pre-existing flakiness;
the test passes in isolation.)

Closes flywheel_connectors-4g0qr.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -254,6 +254,39 @@ impl StoredObject {
             key,
         ))
     }
+
+    /// Structural validation that does not require an `ObjectIdKey`.
+    ///
+    /// `ObjectId` is a *keyed* derivation (HMAC-style with the zone's
+    /// `ObjectIdKey`), so the storage layer cannot independently verify
+    /// `self.object_id == derive_id(self.header, self.body, key)` without
+    /// access to the zone keys (which live in `ZoneKeyMaterial`, outside
+    /// the store). Full content-ID verification is the runtime-API layer's
+    /// responsibility (e.g., MeshNode at the put boundary).
+    ///
+    /// This method provides the strongest *key-free* check available:
+    /// run the canonical encoding pipeline that `derive_id` would run, so
+    /// that any object which could not have been produced by a legitimate
+    /// `derive_id` call is rejected. It catches:
+    ///
+    ///   - Headers that fail canonical-CBOR encoding (NaN/Infinity floats,
+    ///     duplicate map keys, non-canonical structure, `serde` errors).
+    ///   - `body` lengths that, combined with the canonical header, exceed
+    ///     [`fcp_cbor::MAX_CANONICAL_OBJECT_BYTES`] (64 MiB) — protects
+    ///     downstream allocators from oversized objects smuggled through a
+    ///     deserialized envelope (e.g., a 500 MiB `Put` in the WAL).
+    ///
+    /// This is a NECESSARY but not SUFFICIENT check for content-addressing
+    /// integrity. Storage backends should call this on every write
+    /// (runtime API + WAL replay + snapshot recovery) for defense in depth.
+    ///
+    /// # Errors
+    /// Returns the same `SerializationError` that `canonical_bytes` would
+    /// return for a malformed or oversized object.
+    pub fn validate_structure(&self) -> Result<(), SerializationError> {
+        Self::canonical_bytes(&self.header, &self.body)?;
+        Ok(())
+    }
 }
 
 #[cfg(test)]
